title: Lesson 334
transcript: All right, let's keep going through our cryptographic attacks here. The next type of cryptographic attack we're gonna talk about here is a temporary file attack. The idea behind this is that any time that, say, a CPU is encrypting and decrypting some information, that CPU is going to need a copy of, obviously, the plain text or the ciphertext, but also a copy of The key. Now typically, the key would be stored in a very secure location, like the TPM module or the HSM module. These are custom hardware that are very locked down, very secure, and very difficult for an attacker to try and extract a key from. But this is where the idea of a temporary file attack comes in. Rather than the attacker trying to break the security of the TPM or the HSM module, the attacker is just going to wait for the point when the CPU is going to perform some encryption and decryption and therefore need to grab a copy of the key and probably store it somewhere in the CPU's cache or registers or in RAM. And these storage locations, cache, registers, RAM, they are not as secure as the TPM module. And so the whole idea behind a temporary file attack is that the attacker waits for some sensitive information, like say the encryption key, to be stored in a less secure temporary location, and then grab a copy of the sensitive data, grab a copy of the key from that temporary file location. That's a temporary file attack. All right, next, let's talk about implementation attacks. The basic idea behind an implementation attack is that the attacker is not actually attacking the cryptographic algorithm itself. The attacker is instead going after weaknesses in how the algorithm has been implemented. That's the whole idea behind an implementation attack is essentially a developer takes an excellent algorithm and then does a really crappy job of implementing it, leaving weaknesses in the system. And let me give you a textbook example of a implementation attack. Anyone remember WEP, Wired Equivalency Protocol? This was a very pervasive, when wireless routers first became a thing, WEP was the protocol that was used to encrypt wireless traffic. This was a very, back in the day, this was an extremely important protocol because this is how you encrypted your wireless traffic. Unfortunately, a major issue was discovered in WEP, and it basically made WEP Totally useless. WEP encryption could be easily broken. Why? Well, it was a perfect example of an implementation attack. And actually, maybe I'll test your knowledge here. See if you know, do you happen to recall what was the algorithm used at the heart of WEP? And I'll give you a hint. It's a stream cipher. RC4. There you go. John knows. RC4. Yeah, exactly. RC4 was the algorithm. It still is, but you shouldn't use it. RC4 is the algorithm used at the heart of web. RC4 is an excellent algorithm. There have been no major issues found with RC4. And yet I'm telling you, WEP, which uses RC4, is totally broken. Why? Well, because the developers of WEP took this excellent algorithm, RC4, and did a terrible job of implementing it. One of the problems is that the initialization vector is Static. Part of it is static and the rest of it is too short. If you remember, right, the initialization vector, it's supposed to be a big random number. That's the whole point of the initialization vector. It should be a large random number. And yet the IV in WEP, part of it's static. And the rest of it's too short. This is one of the issues. There's a couple of others, but this is one of the major issues with web, or sorry, with how RC4 has been implemented in web. Perfect example of an implementation attack. And by the way, you should remember all of these. You do need to know about wired equivalency protocol. John will talk about it more in domain four. You do need to know that the algorithm used within WEP is RC4. And you do need to know that this is a perfect example of an implementation attack. And specifically, that the specific problem you need to know about is that the initialization vector is too short and part of it is static. And there's one other piece that you should remember related to this. When this was first found, this was a massive problem. Everyone was using WEP and that meant that none of the wireless traffic was secure. And the proper solution was to create and ratify new protocols. And so that work got underway right away to create and ratify WPA and then WPA2. But that was going to take a long time. And so a temporary bandaid solution needed to be found to deal with this WEP fiasco. And that temporary bandaid was TKIP, the Temporal Key Integrity Protocol. So remember those four related to each other, right? WEP is very insecure, despite the fact that it uses the excellent RC4, perfect example of an implementation attack. And the band-aid solution to deal with this fiasco was TKIP, Temporal Key Integrity Protocol. Okay, next type of cryptographic attack, fault injection. The whole idea behind fault injection is basically just to F up a system. It's not to try and get access to sensitive data, it's nothing like that. It's basically just to mess a system up, to cause corruption of the data, corruption of the system, maybe even crash the system. That's the whole purpose of fault injection attacks. And there's quite a number of interesting ways that you can cause faults in systems. One of the very obvious ones is just mess with the power going into the system. If you mess with the voltage going into a sensitive computer system, it's gonna cause faults in the system. So that's the idea of voltage glitching. The next one here is quite interesting. Clock glitching. The idea here is that you mess with the system clock time. You either, you know, you basically just change the time. You might wonder like, well, why is this going to cause issues? Well, especially related to cryptography, the system clock time is often very important. If you mess with the system time while a system is performing various cryptographic operations, it will very likely cause falls. So that's the idea with clock glitching. Mess with the system time. When a system is performing encryption or decryption, it will often cause faults. Laser injection is basically the idea that you point lasers at something. If you point lasers at something, it will excite electrons. You get errant electrons in a... In an electronic system, it's gonna cause faults. So that's laser injection. And then the final one is an electromagnetic injection. Tons of the components within our computer systems, hard drives being a perfect example, rely on magnetism. If you put a strong magnetic field near a computer system, it's often gonna cause pretty major faults. So these are all different ways of causing faults in a computer system. All right, the next cryptographic attack we're gonna talk about are side channel attacks. And the name explains what this is. These are attacks where the attacker doesn't, they're not logged into the system that they're attacking. Instead, they're kind of attacking from the side of the system. And these are usually essentially kind of hardware attacks. So, Let's start with timing and power attacks. These are very closely related. And by the way, these are extremely sophisticated attacks that require really precision hardware. These are very sophisticated attacks. This is not some dude, some person in their parents' basement doing this. This is nation state level stuff, perhaps kind of university researchers. So let's start with a timing attack. The idea behind a timing attack is that the attacker very, very precisely monitors a CPU. And specifically what they're monitoring is exactly how long certain calculations are taking. So quick, what's two plus two? There we go. Okay, that was pretty quick. All right, what's 176 billion times 209? Four. Quick but wrong. 42. The meaning of life, also wrong. Okay, but the point is, right, it's gonna take you a lot more time to perform that calculation. And this is the idea of a timing attack. If you vary, precisely measure exactly how long the CPU is taking to perform certain calculations, that gives you clues as to the numbers involved. Bigger numbers, it's gonna take a little more time. Smaller numbers, it's gonna take a little less time. And so if you're able to measure precisely enough, over a long enough period of time, apply statistical analysis, you can start to suss out what the numbers might be involved in, so in say a cryptographic calculation. And that's the idea of a timing attack. You might actually be able to figure out say the encryption key just by measuring exactly how long something takes. And to give this a more specific example and to maybe test your knowledge, Think about DES, right? John talked about the data encryption standard. How many rounds of substitution and transposition does DES do? Ah, I was going to say don't answer, John. Because I know you know. Yeah, 16, right? DES does 16 rounds of substitution and transposition. So if you very precisely measure exactly how long each of those steps are taking, that can give you clues as to say what the encryption key might be. So that's a timing attack. Very closely related to this is a power attack. Same sort of setup. You're very precisely monitoring the CPU, but instead of measuring how long a certain calculation takes, you're measuring how much power it consumes. And this exact same idea. Bigger numbers are gonna consume more power. Smaller numbers are gonna consume less power for each calculation. And so you measure precisely enough over a long enough period of time, apply statistical analysis, you might be able to figure out sensitive information like the key. The other side channel attack listed here is radiation emissions. And this is the idea that, again, it kind of goes back to like what we talked about with emanations. our modern day computers and any computer system is going to emanate all sorts of different electromagnetic waves and if you have sensitive enough equipment and especially if you're close enough you can potentially detect some of these emissions and therefore an attacker could detect some of these emissions electromagnetic emissions from a computer system and allowed them to gather sensitive information that you wouldn't want them to. And this is a type of side channel attack because of course they don't have direct access. They're not logged into the system. Instead, they're kind of listening in from the side to pick up, detect some of these radiation emissions. And actually, I'll just share this, not gonna be on the exam, but I think this is a really cool real world example of side channel attacks. So there's this, a university researcher group discovered this first attack that got called the really cool name Row Hammer. And this relates to memory. Oversimplifying here. The way that data is stored in memory, in RAM, is essentially in rows and that's why this is called the row hammer attack so you have rows of memory and remember memory segmentation each application is going to have its own little bit of memory space and each application should only be able to access its own memory space and not you know another memory space that might belong to another application idea behind the row hammer attack is what researchers discovered is that you've got these rows of memory the rows of memory are really close together because you're trying to pack as much data as you can into ram and what they discovered is that if you quickly really quickly write write write write write data to a particular row of memory because the rows are so so close together if you write enough memory Eventually, you're going to get what's known as a bit flip, where writing data to this row of memory is actually going to cause one of the bits of memory in this row of memory to flip from, say, a 0 to a 1 or a 1 to a 0. And I mean, statistically, this is very rare. So this is why you have to hammer away. This is why it's called Row Hammer, because you hammer away. You write, write, write, write, write, write data to this memory space, and maybe even the one below it. You write, write, write, write, write data higher. And if you do this enough, if you hammer away at this memory space and this memory space, eventually you're gonna cause bit flips in this bit of memory space, in this row of memory, corrupting the target row of memory. So that's why it's called a row hammer attack, and it causes integrity issues. You can corrupt another application's data without having direct access to its memory space, which is why it's an example of a side channel attack. Super cool. even cooler, again, not on the exam, but pretty cool, is something known as RAM bleed. The same group of researchers extended this, and what they realized is that when you're, like say you're hammering away and you're writing data to this row, not only are you eventually gonna cause some bit flips in this row of memory, but based on the data that's in this row of memory, that will cause some bit flips in the row of memory that you're hammering away in. And so what you basically do in a RAM bleed attack is you hammer, hammer, hammer your writing data here, which is not only gonna cause some bit flips in this row, but it'll also cause some bit flips in the row that you're writing to. And based on statistical analysis, you can figure out like there's a higher probability, like let's say there's a one in this row, that that will cause a bit flip to say a one in this row. Or if it's a zero in this row, that'll cause a bit flip to a zero in this row. The specifics are obviously beyond this discussion here, but the idea is that by hammering away and writing data to this row, the data in this row will cause certain bit flips in this row. And by doing this enough, you can actually figure out, you can read the data, in a row of memory that you have no access to. And that's why RAM bleed is actually a confidentiality attack. And when this was first discovered, it freaked a lot of people out because this was a way that an attacker, like say in the public cloud, could read another customer's memory space. So pretty cool example of a side channel attack.
